perturbation.original_text: Both parties agree to maintain complete confidentiality concerning the business affairs of the other and all proprietary information which each may receive from the other.
perturbation.changed_text: Both parties agree to maintain confidentiality concerning the business affairs of the other and all information which each may receive from the other.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the term 'proprietary' from 'proprietary information' broadens the scope of confidentiality beyond trade secrets and confidential commercial information, potentially encompassing any information, which could be problematic and unenforceable. It creates ambiguity because confidentiality is now subject to interpretation, blurring the lines of what is legally protectable.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA defines a trade secret as information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known and is subject to reasonable efforts to maintain its secrecy. By removing 'proprietary', the contract attempts to protect all information, which is inconsistent with trade secret laws.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Uniform Trade Secrets Act - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Uniform act in the United States Intellectual property Authors' rights Copyleft Copyright Database right Farmers' rights Geographical indication Indigenous intellectual property Industrial design right Integrated circuit layout design protection Intellectual property infringement Literary property Moral rights Patent Peasants' rights Plant breeders' rights Plant genetic resources Proprietary software Related rights Supplementary protection certificate Trade dress Trade secret Trademark Utility model Patent infringement Related topics Abandonware Artificial intelligence and copyright Brand protection Copyright abolition Copyright troll Criticism of copyright Bioprospecting Biopiracy Idea–expression distinction Limitations and exceptions to copyright Fair dealing Fair use Paraphrasing Right to quote Orphan work Patent troll Pirate Party Public domain Outline of intellectual property Outline of patents Higher categories: Property and Property law v t e The Uniform Trade Secrets Act ( UTSA ), published by the Uniform Law Commission (ULC) in 1979 and amended in 1985, is a model law designed for adoption by U.S. states . [ 1 ] It was developed to resolve inconsistencies in the treatment of trade secrets across different states. [ 1 ] (p1) The UTSA provides unitary definitions for "trade secret" and "trade secret misappropriation" and establishes a single statute of limitations, replacing the varied property, quasi-contractual, and fiduciary liability frameworks previously applied under common law . [ 1 ] (p2) To date, 49 states, the District of Columbia, Puerto Rico, and the U.S. Virgin Islands have adopted the UTSA, with New York as the remaining exceptions. [ 2 ] Motivation [ edit ] A prefatory note to the UTSA states some original motivations for the act: A valid patent provides a legal monopoly for seventeen years in exchange for public disclosure of an invention. If, however, the courts ultimately decide that the Patent Office improperly issued a patent, an invention has been disclosed to competitors with no corresponding benefit. In view of the substantial number of patents that the courts invalidate, many businesses now elect to protect commercially valuable information by relying on the state trade secret protection law. — Uniform Trade Secrets Act, Prefatory Note [ 3 ] The UTSA made note of the commercial value and competitive advantages inherent in trade secrets. Unlike patent protection, which was addressed at the federal level, trade secret misappropriation was addressed at the state level. [ 3 ] In the United States there existed a prevalence of interstate commercial transactions that extended beyond the jurisdiction of individual state legislation. For example, goods may have been manufactured in State A, warehoused in State B, sold from State C, and delivered in State D. As a result, the UTSA sought to alleviate the uneven development and "uncertainty concerning the parameters of trade secret protection" by recommending a uniform trade secret law and, at the same time, allowing the states the flexibility to meet local circumstances by modifying the text as enacted in eac
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes 'proprietary' from 'proprietary information,' broadening the confidentiality scope. The Law Explanation states this is inconsistent with trade secret laws because the UTSA defines a trade secret as deriving independent economic value from not being generally known and subject to reasonable secrecy efforts. By protecting 'all information', it overreaches trade secret laws. Scraped Law Snippet 2 supports this by referencing the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA) which aims to resolve inconsistencies in trade secret treatment, providing definitions for 'trade secret' and 'trade secret misappropriation'. The original contract focused on 'proprietary information,' aligning with trade secret law's intent to protect valuable, secret information. The changed text's broader scope contradicts this by attempting to protect information beyond legally recognized trade secrets, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Both parties agree not to circumvent each other in dealing with the contacts of the other.
perturbation.changed_text: Both parties agree not to directly compete with each other by utilizing contacts made available by the other during the term of this agreement. Any activity after the termination of this agreement is permissible.
perturbation.explanation: This changes the restriction from a non-circumvention agreement to a non-compete clause that is limited to the duration of the agreement. This could be deemed unreasonable if the original intent was broader. The new clause explicitly permits competition post-termination.
perturbation.law_explanation: California law generally prohibits non-compete agreements, except in very limited circumstances such as the sale of a business. The original clause likely aimed to prevent unfair competition stemming directly from the confidential relationship. However, the modified clause creates an explicit non-compete during the term and then allows it post-term, which could violate California's strong public policy against restraints of trade.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a non-compete clause limited to the agreement's term, explicitly permitting competition post-termination, which contradicts California Business and Professions Code Section 16600. The original non-circumvention clause aimed to prevent unfair competition from the confidential relationship. BPC 16600(a) states, "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." The changed text creates a restraint of trade during the agreement term that could be seen as violating this statute and creates an explicit allowance for post-term activities. While the original text also restrains trade, the explicit introduction of a time-limited non-compete clause introduces a clear violation, and the misaligned terminology creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: All notices of discharge, termination or discontinuance of service, by the other party, must be submitted in written form and sent by certified mail or verified e-mail to the address of record of the notified party.
perturbation.changed_text: All notices of discharge, termination or discontinuance of service, by the other party, must be submitted electronically.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirements for 'certified mail' and specifying only 'electronically', the provision eliminates legally recognized methods of providing notice, potentially causing uncertainty and disputes in the event of termination. Certain jurisdictions require specific notice methods for legal validity.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act (E-Sign Act)
perturbation.law_explanation: While the E-Sign Act generally validates electronic signatures and records, it also contains consumer protection provisions and does not automatically override specific requirements in other laws that mandate particular methods of delivery (like certified mail for certain legal notices). By mandating only electronic notice, the revised clause could conflict with other statutes or regulations requiring physical delivery for specific types of termination notices.

perturbation.justification: The changed text mandates electronic submission, eliminating the original contract's provision for certified mail, a legally recognized method of providing notice. The Law Explanation suggests this could conflict with statutes requiring physical delivery for specific termination notices, potentially undermining legal validity where such requirements exist. The scraped law snippet from GovInfo and the Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act indicate that while electronic signatures are generally validated, they do not override specific delivery method requirements in other laws. By removing the certified mail option, the contract creates a contradiction where physical delivery is legally required.

perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees to negotiate directly and solely with and through the other to any new potential financing, joint ventures, mergers, acquisition or other opportunities, which one makes known to the other, and each shall specify the particular capital sources and/or project.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees to negotiate directly with the other party to any new potential financing, joint ventures, mergers, acquisition or other opportunities, which one makes known to the other.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'and solely with and through the other' eliminates the exclusivity aspect of the negotiation agreement. This now allows a party to potentially negotiate deals independently after initially informing the other party. It could be interpreted as removing the obligation to complete the deal through the other party, undermining the purpose of the original clause.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law - Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: Most jurisdictions recognize an implied duty of good faith and fair dealing in contracts. While the original clause established a clear expectation of exclusive negotiation, removing the exclusivity element creates ambiguity. If a party informs the other of an opportunity, then bypasses them to complete the deal independently, it could be argued that they violated the implied duty of good faith, even though the modified contract technically allows for it. The implied duty of good faith prevents parties from acting in bad faith to undermine the benefits of the agreement, even if the contract language is technically compliant.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modification removes the exclusivity aspect of negotiations. The law explanation states that removing the exclusivity element creates ambiguity and could violate the implied duty of good faith. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines 'good faith' as encompassing honest dealing and observance of fair dealing standards. The original contract ensured exclusive dealing, aligning with good faith. By removing 'and solely with and through the other,' the changed text permits actions that could be construed as undermining the agreement, potentially violating the duty of good faith.
